mappings.rationaleAI: Adam Smith's foundational treatise directly established core economic concepts including division of labour, free markets, wealth accumulation, and the productive power of self-interest, while advancing arguments for limited government intervention that define the private enterprise model and Western market economies.

Ricardo's Theory of Comparative Advantage is one of the foundational concepts of economics (A.7), and his arguments for free trade and analysis of how existing policy favoured landlords address competition (A.6) and the functioning of Western-type market economies (C.2).

The Physiocrats developed early systematic economic theory including the laissez-faire doctrine and Quesnay's Tableau Economique modelling economic circulation, addressing scarce resource allocation (A.1), the theoretical foundations of the private enterprise economy (C.1.a), and their agrarian theory of value represents a distinctive early economic system (C.5).

Hayek's central argument that centralized economic planning threatens liberty directly compares the pure private enterprise model (C.1.a) against centrally planned socialist economies (C.1.b), while his influence on Thatcherism and Reaganomics shaped Western market economies (C.2) and debates over mixed economies (C.1.c).

The gold standard's role in monetary policy, exchange rates, and international capital flows is a core macroeconomic topic (B.3), and its constraining effect during the Great Depression illustrates the functioning and vulnerabilities of Western-type market economies (C.2).

Mechanized production and factory organization exemplify the division of labour (A.2), available capital driving industrialization illustrates the concept of capital (A.5), Britain's economic transformation demonstrates growth and development (A.8), and the revolution established the template for Western market economies (C.2).

Directly addresses whether democracy is capitalism's truest conduit, Friedman's defence of free-market efficiency (C.1.a), Sen's work on economic justice in developing countries (C.4), and the tension between globalized capital and democratic institutions in mixed economies (C.1.c).

Game theory's analysis of strategic decision-making in market competition and negotiation directly addresses the concept of competition (A.6), and its modelling of rational choice by individuals and firms is a core tool of microeconomic analysis (B.1).

Malthus's argument that population grows geometrically while food supply increases arithmetically is a foundational statement of scarcity in economics (A.1), his theory of natural population checks embodies diminishing returns (A.3), and neo-Malthusian concerns about resource limits address growth and development (A.8).

The comparison of why modern economic development arose in Europe rather than China involves property rights, competitive state systems versus unified bureaucracy, and differing relationships between state power and mercantile enterprise, directly addressing comparative economic systems (C.2, C.5) and conditions for growth (A.8).

Lenin's founding of the Soviet state, development of Marxist-Leninist ideology, and implementation of the New Economic Policy represent the creation and early operation of the centrally planned socialist economy (C.1.b) and the archetypal Soviet-type economic system (C.3).

British government responses shaped by laissez-faire economics and Poor Law administration illustrate the consequences of applying pure private enterprise principles (C.1.a) within a Western market economy (C.2), serving as a case study in free-market ideology's real-world operation.

The 1834 reform driven by Malthusian economics and the belief that relief discouraged labour embodies private enterprise logic (C.1.a), while the workhouse system's eventual replacement by the welfare state illustrates evolution toward mixed economies with varying degrees of planning (C.1.c).

Fukuyama's analysis of the transition from industrial to information-based economies addresses economic growth and structural transformation (A.8), and the comparison with earlier agrarian-to-industrial transitions illustrates how mixed economies evolve through technological change (C.1.c).
